learning_objectives: Classify game mechanics into functional categories (physics, economy, progression, social, information)
Distinguish core mechanics from secondary and meta-mechanics using formal criteria
Analyze how a single core mechanic generates diverse player experiences across different game contexts
Map the mechanical DNA of a game using a verb-based decomposition
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 1-2
A Game Design Vocabulary by Anthropy & Clark, Ch. 3
Characteristics of Games by Elias, Garfield & Gutschera, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction:
Welcome to Game Theory and Mechanics. Building on the foundations from GD101, this course examines the formal systems that make games work, from the smallest mechanical interaction to large-scale progression arcs. Where GD101 gave you the vocabulary to talk about games, this course gives you the mathematical and structural tools to build, analyze, and fix them.

Every game is composed of mechanics, the rules and systems that define what players can do and what happens when they do it. But mechanics are not all the same kind of thing. Some govern physics (how objects move and collide), some govern economics (how resources flow between entities), some govern progression (how players advance through content), some govern social interaction (how players communicate and collaborate), and some govern information (what players know and when they learn it). Understanding these categories helps you design with precision.

Today we build a taxonomy of mechanical types and practice decomposing games into their mechanical components. We will use a verb-based approach: every mechanic can be expressed as a verb that a player performs (jump, trade, build, negotiate, match, aim). Mapping all the verbs in a game reveals its mechanical DNA and shows which verbs carry the most weight in the player experience.
key_concepts: Core mechanic: the single repeatable action that defines moment-to-moment gameplay; the verb the player performs most frequently
Secondary mechanics: actions that support, modify, or contextualize the core (power-ups, scoring, equipment selection)
Meta-mechanics: systems operating above individual play sessions (rankings, seasonal progression, achievement systems, unlockable content)
Physics mechanics: movement, collision, gravity, projectile trajectories, and spatial relationships
Economy mechanics: resource generation, consumption, exchange, storage, and conversion between resource types
Progression mechanics: experience points, levels, skill trees, ability unlocks, and content gating
Social mechanics: communication, trading, voting, alliance formation, betrayal, and cooperative actions
Information mechanics: fog of war, hidden information, signal reading, deduction, and information asymmetry
Verb-based decomposition: expressing every player action as a verb and ranking verbs by frequency and importance
Atomic game design: composing complex systems from simple, well-defined mechanical atoms that interact predictably
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side mechanical decomposition of Pac-Man (navigate, consume, evade), Tetris (rotate, place, clear), and Civilization VI (explore, settle, research, build, negotiate, conquer), categorizing each verb by mechanical type and identifying core vs. secondary mechanics.
setup_requirements: Design journal (continuation from GD101 or new notebook)
Access to at least two different games for hands-on mechanical analysis
Mechanical taxonomy reference sheet (provided)

review.value: The fundamental, repeatable player action around which the game is built. Changing the core mechanic changes the game's identity. Identified by asking: what does the player do most often?
Physics (spatial), Economy (resource), Progression (advancement), Social (interaction), and Information (knowledge) mechanics. Most games combine mechanics from multiple categories.
Listing every player action as a verb, then ranking by frequency and impact. Reveals which mechanics carry the most weight in the experience and which are underutilized.
Building complex systems from simple, well-defined mechanical atoms. Each atom should be understandable in isolation; complexity arises from their interactions.

learning_objectives: Diagram positive and negative feedback loops in any game system using formal notation
Predict the behavioral consequences of each loop type on match pacing and player experience
Design a simple system that uses both loop types intentionally to achieve a specific pacing goal
Identify hidden feedback loops that are not immediately apparent to the player
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 4
Characteristics of Games by Elias, Garfield & Gutschera, Ch. 7
Thinking in Systems by Donella Meadows, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction:
Feedback loops are the invisible engines that drive game pacing. Every game is a system, and systems are defined by their feedback structures. Understanding feedback loops is essential for controlling whether a game snowballs toward a decisive conclusion or rubber-bands toward a close finish.

A positive feedback loop occurs when success breeds more success. In Monopoly, owning properties generates rent, which funds buying more properties, which generates more rent. The result is accelerating divergence between players: the rich get richer, the poor get poorer, and the game drags on long after the outcome is decided. A negative feedback loop occurs when success triggers counterforces that reduce the advantage. In Mario Kart, players in first place receive weak items while players in last place receive powerful items, compressing the field and keeping races competitive.

Neither type is inherently good or bad. Positive loops create decisive, climactic endings and reward early skill. Negative loops create tense, competitive experiences and give losing players hope. The design question is always: what emotional arc do you want for your players, and which combination of loops produces it? Most well-paced games use both types strategically: positive loops for short-term excitement within a round or encounter, and negative loops for long-term balance across the entire session.
key_concepts: Positive feedback loop: a reinforcing cycle where advantage compounds (output feeds back as amplified input). Destabilizing, accelerating, creates decisive outcomes.
Negative feedback loop: a balancing cycle where advantage triggers counterforces (output feeds back as dampening input). Stabilizing, equilibrium-seeking, extends competition.
Formal loop diagramming: nodes (game states), edges (causal relationships), and polarity labels (+/- on each edge) to map system behavior
Runaway leader problem: when unchecked positive feedback makes the outcome inevitable long before the game ends, wasting losing players' time
Rubber-banding frustration: when excessive negative feedback makes skilled play feel pointless, punishing leaders for playing well
Combining loops: using positive loops for short-term excitement (winning a battle) within a negative loop for long-term balance (catch-up mechanisms across the war)
Hidden feedback: loops embedded in economic or social systems that players experience indirectly (e.g., reputation systems where helpful behavior attracts more help)
Feedback loop strength: loops can be weak (small influence) or strong (dominant driver), and strength can change over the course of a game
System dynamics modeling: using simple mathematical models to predict loop behavior over time (stock-and-flow diagrams)
Case study: analyzing the feedback structures of Monopoly, Mario Kart, and League of Legends to explain their radically different pacing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a simple economy game with tokens and tracking the score divergence over 10 rounds, then adding a wealth redistribution mechanic (negative feedback) and replaying to observe how the pacing changes.
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet application for modeling feedback behavior numerically
Tokens and score sheets for in-class simulation
System dynamics notation reference sheet (provided)

review.value: Success breeds more success. Destabilizing, accelerating, creates decisive outcomes. Risk: runaway leaders and boring endgames for losing players.
Success triggers counterforces that reduce the advantage. Stabilizing, equilibrium-seeking, keeps competition close. Risk: skilled play feels unrewarded.
Formal notation using nodes and signed edges to map feedback structures. Essential for predicting system behavior before building.
The best-paced games layer positive loops (short-term excitement) within negative loops (long-term balance). The ratio determines the emotional arc.

class_schedule.topic: Game Balance Theory: Symmetry and Asymmetry
learning_objectives: Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric balance with formal criteria
Apply transitive and intransitive relationship models to balance analysis
Calculate cost-efficiency ratios for asymmetric game elements using spreadsheet modeling
Identify dominant strategies and propose mechanical fixes
readings: Game Balance by Schreiber & Brathwaite, Ch. 1-4
Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 6
Sirlin's Playing to Win, Ch. 4-5 (balance and counter-play)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Balance is the art of making a game fair without making it boring. A perfectly balanced game where every option is identical offers no interesting choices. A badly balanced game where one option dominates offers only one choice. The sweet spot is a game where multiple viable strategies exist, each with distinct strengths and weaknesses, so that the player's skill in choosing and adapting their strategy determines the outcome.

Symmetric balance gives all players identical starting conditions and options. Chess is symmetrically balanced: both players have the same pieces, with only the first-move advantage breaking perfect symmetry. Asymmetric balance gives players different abilities, units, or resources while maintaining roughly equal chances of winning. StarCraft's Terran, Protoss, and Zerg factions each play completely differently, but competitive data shows win rates hovering near 50% for each matchup at the highest skill levels. Asymmetric balance is harder to achieve but produces more varied and replayable games.

The two fundamental relationship types in balance are transitive and intransitive. Transitive relationships form strict hierarchies: a Steel Sword is always better than an Iron Sword. Intransitive relationships form circular dominance: Rock beats Scissors, Scissors beats Paper, Paper beats Rock. Transitive relationships reward investment and create satisfying progression. Intransitive relationships prevent dominant strategies and encourage adaptation. Most well-balanced games use both.
key_concepts: Symmetric balance: identical starting conditions and options for all players (Chess, Go, Pong)
Asymmetric balance: different abilities, units, or resources with equivalent winning probability (StarCraft factions, Overwatch heroes)
Transitive relationships: strict power hierarchy where A is always better than B (upgrade paths, equipment tiers)
Intransitive relationships: circular dominance where A beats B, B beats C, C beats A (type matchups, counter-picking)
Cost curves: assigning numerical costs to game elements proportional to their power, then checking for outliers
Dominant strategies: options that are always optimal regardless of context; their existence means meaningful choice has collapsed
Nash equilibrium: a state where no player can improve their outcome by changing strategy alone; the stable point of competitive systems
Perceived fairness vs. mathematical fairness: players judge balance subjectively; a mathematically fair system that feels unfair is a design failure
The balance process: internal playtesting, community feedback, data collection, incremental tuning, and continuous monitoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Spreadsheet analysis of StarCraft II unit cost-efficiency: calculating damage-per-resource-spent for Terran Marine, Protoss Zealot, and Zerg Zergling, then modeling 100 simulated encounters to find statistical balance outliers.
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet application for cost-curve modeling
Calculator for efficiency ratios
Unit data reference sheet (provided)

review.value: Different abilities with equivalent winning chances. Harder to achieve than symmetric balance but produces more varied, replayable experiences.
Circular dominance (A beats B, B beats C, C beats A) that prevents any single option from being universally dominant. The foundation of counter-play.
When one option is always best regardless of context, meaningful choice collapses. Identifying and eliminating dominant strategies is the core balance task.
Plotting the power of each game element against its cost reveals outliers: elements that give too much power for their cost (overpowered) or too little (underpowered).

learning_objectives: Design reward schedules using principles from behavioral psychology with precise timing
Calculate expected value and variance for probability-based reward systems
Evaluate the ethical implications of variable-ratio reward schedules in commercial games
Implement a multi-layered reward system that combines short-term and long-term incentives
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 8
Actionable Gamification by Yu-kai Chou, Ch. 4-5
Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products by Nir Eyal, Ch. 3 (Variable Reward)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Rewards are a game's way of communicating value, acknowledging progress, and shaping future behavior. How and when you deliver rewards profoundly affects what players do, how long they play, and whether they come back. Every reward decision is an implicit bet about human psychology.

Behavioral psychology gives us four fundamental reinforcement schedules. Fixed-ratio rewards come after a set number of actions (every 100 kills, you level up). Variable-ratio rewards come after an unpredictable number of actions (each enemy has a 3% chance of dropping a rare item). Fixed-interval rewards come after a set time period (daily login bonus). Variable-interval rewards come at unpredictable times (a random event triggers during gameplay). Each schedule produces different player behavior patterns, and understanding those patterns lets you design with intention.

The most psychologically potent schedule is variable-ratio reinforcement. It produces the highest sustained response rate and the strongest resistance to extinction (players keep going long after rewards stop). This is the same mechanism that makes slot machines addictive, which raises serious ethical questions about its use in games, particularly those with real-money transactions. Today we explore both the design power and the ethical responsibilities of reward system design.
key_concepts: Fixed-ratio schedules: reward after every N actions (XP per kill, crafting recipes); predictable, steady engagement, low excitement per reward
Variable-ratio schedules: reward after unpredictable action counts (loot drops, gacha pulls); highest engagement rate, strongest habit formation, ethical concerns
Fixed-interval schedules: reward after set time periods (daily login rewards, weekly challenges); drives regular return visits, diminishing excitement over time
Variable-interval schedules: reward at unpredictable times (random world events, surprise gifts); maintains alertness and exploration behavior
Expected value: E(X) = sum of (probability * value) for each outcome; the long-run average payoff of a random event
Variance: how much individual outcomes scatter around the expected value; high variance feels exciting but can frustrate
Layered reward systems: combining immediate feedback (score popups), session rewards (end-of-match loot), and long-term progression (seasonal rank) to sustain engagement at multiple timescales
Reward timing: the dopamine spike occurs at the moment of anticipation, not receipt; build-up, suspense, and reveal sequences amplify perceived reward value
The ethical spectrum: from transparent rewards (you see exactly what you earn) to exploitative systems (paid random loot boxes targeting vulnerable players)
Pity timers and bad-luck protection: guaranteeing a rare reward after N unsuccessful attempts to reduce frustration without eliminating randomness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a loot-drop probability table with expected value and variance calculations, then simulating 100 drops to compare theoretical distributions with actual results and discussing the emotional impact of streaks and droughts.
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet or calculator for probability modeling
Dice sets (d6, d10, d20, d100) for physical simulation
Probability reference sheet (provided)

review.value: Rewards after unpredictable action counts. Produces the highest response rate and strongest habit formation. Same mechanism as slot machines, carrying ethical responsibility.
The long-run average outcome: E(X) = sum(probability * value). Essential for calibrating reward generosity and ensuring the player economy is sustainable.
Combining immediate, session-level, and long-term rewards sustains engagement across multiple timescales. Each layer addresses a different psychological need.
The line between engagement and exploitation is real. Designers must consider vulnerable players, transparency of odds, and the relationship between reward systems and real-money spending.

learning_objectives: Calculate probabilities for common game scenarios (dice, cards, loot tables) using combinatorics
Distinguish between input randomness and output randomness and their effects on player agency
Design random systems that feel fair to players despite mathematical variance
Apply pseudo-random distribution techniques to reduce frustration without eliminating randomness
readings: Characteristics of Games by Elias, Garfield & Gutschera, Ch. 4-6
Game Balance by Schreiber & Brathwaite, Ch. 5-6
Probability and Games: A Mathematical Approach (online resource, MIT OpenCourseWare)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Randomness is one of the most powerful and misunderstood tools in game design. Used well, it creates surprise, replayability, and dramatic tension. Used poorly, it makes players feel that their decisions do not matter and that outcomes are arbitrary.

The key distinction is between input randomness and output randomness. Input randomness occurs before the player makes a decision: a randomly generated map, a shuffled deck, a random starting position. The player sees the random result and then makes an informed choice about how to respond. Output randomness occurs after the player makes a decision: a dice roll determines whether an attack hits, a random number decides if a spell succeeds. Output randomness can feel unfair because the player committed to an action and then the game overruled their decision with a coin flip.

Both types are valid design tools, but they serve different purposes. Input randomness creates varied starting conditions that reward adaptability (each game of Slay the Spire starts with a different card offering). Output randomness creates dramatic uncertainty and models the real-world unpredictability that makes simulations feel alive (a 95% hit chance in XCOM still misses one time in twenty). The design challenge is managing the emotional impact of randomness so that it enhances rather than undermines the player's sense of agency.
key_concepts: Basic probability: P(event) = favorable outcomes / total outcomes; independent vs. dependent events; the multiplication rule for sequential events
Combinatorics for games: permutations (order matters) and combinations (order does not) for calculating hand probabilities in card games
Input randomness: random elements resolved before the player decides (random map generation, card draw); feels fair because the player responds to known information
Output randomness: random elements resolved after the player decides (hit/miss rolls, critical chance); can feel unfair if high stakes depend on the roll
Perceived probability vs. actual probability: players overestimate rare events and underestimate common ones (a 10% miss feels like it happens all the time)
Pseudo-random distribution: algorithms that reduce variance without eliminating randomness (Dota 2's PRD makes each successive failure more likely to succeed, smoothing outcomes)
Pity timers: guaranteeing a positive outcome after N failures, providing a frustration floor while maintaining randomness for most interactions
The gambler's fallacy: the mistaken belief that past random outcomes influence future ones; players expect balance in small samples even though randomness does not work that way
Seed-based randomness: using deterministic seeds for reproducibility in procedural generation and replays
Designing for drama: using randomness to create stories rather than to determine outcomes; the 'almost died' moment is more memorable than smooth predictability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulating 50 XCOM-style attacks at 80% hit chance using dice, calculating the actual hit rate, identifying streaks, and discussing why the results 'feel' less than 80% despite the math. Then implementing a pseudo-random distribution algorithm in a spreadsheet and comparing its streak behavior.
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet application for probability simulations
Dice sets for physical probability experiments
Scientific calculator or phone calculator for combinatorics

review.value: Input randomness (before decision) feels fair because players respond to known information. Output randomness (after decision) can feel unfair because the game overrides player choices.
An algorithm that increases the probability of success with each consecutive failure, ensuring that long unlucky streaks are extremely rare while maintaining overall statistical rates.
Players overestimate rare events and underestimate common ones. An 80% hit chance will produce miss streaks that feel far worse than the math suggests.
The mistaken belief that past outcomes influence future ones in independent events. Five misses in a row does not make the next shot more likely to hit.

learning_objectives: Model a closed-loop game economy with sources, sinks, converters, and inventories
Identify inflation, deflation, and stagnation in game economies and propose corrective mechanics
Design a dual-currency system that balances free and premium player progression
Apply stock-and-flow diagramming to visualize resource movement through a game economy
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 5
Virtual Economies: Design and Analysis by Lehdonvirta & Castronova, Ch. 3-5
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 13 (Game Economies)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Every game has an economy, even games that do not seem like they have one. An economy is any system where resources are generated, consumed, stored, and exchanged. Gold coins are resources, but so are ammunition, health points, building materials, time, attention, and social capital. Understanding economic principles lets you control the flow of value through your game and ensure that the player always has meaningful decisions about resource allocation.

A game economy has four fundamental components: sources (where resources enter the system), sinks (where resources leave the system), converters (where one resource type transforms into another), and inventories (where resources are stored). The balance between sources and sinks determines whether the economy inflates (too many resources, nothing feels valuable), deflates (too few resources, everything feels punishing), or reaches a sustainable equilibrium.

Modern free-to-play games add another layer of complexity: dual currencies. A free (soft) currency earned through play and a premium (hard) currency purchased with real money. The design challenge is ensuring that free players can progress meaningfully while premium currency provides convenience or cosmetic value rather than competitive advantage. Getting this balance wrong alienates either paying customers (who feel their purchase is worthless) or free players (who feel locked out of content).
key_concepts: Sources: points where resources enter the game economy (quest rewards, resource nodes, loot drops, time-based generation)
Sinks: points where resources permanently leave the economy (item durability, consumables, upgrade costs, taxes/fees)
Converters: mechanisms that transform one resource type into another (crafting systems, trading, currency exchange)
Inventories: storage locations with capacity constraints that create resource management decisions (bag space, bank limits)
Stock-and-flow diagrams: visual notation showing resource quantities (stocks) and movement rates (flows) through the economy
Inflation: when sources outpace sinks, resource value drops, and prices rise; the feeling that rewards are meaningless
Deflation: when sinks outpace sources, resources become scarce, and new players face insurmountable barriers
Closed vs. open economies: whether resources only flow between system-controlled entities or also between players (player trading)
Dual-currency design: balancing free (earned) and premium (purchased) currencies so both have value without creating pay-to-win
The faucet-drain model: treating sources as faucets and sinks as drains; adjusting flow rates to maintain healthy resource levels over time
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a stock-and-flow diagram for a hypothetical RPG economy (gold sources: quest rewards, monster drops, selling items; gold sinks: equipment, potions, fast travel, housing tax), then simulating 50 play-hours in a spreadsheet to check for inflation or deflation.
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet application for economic modeling
Stock-and-flow diagramming tool or graph paper
Design journal for economy design exercise

review.value: Resources enter through sources and leave through sinks. If sources exceed sinks, the economy inflates. If sinks exceed sources, it deflates. Balance requires careful tuning of both.
Visual notation showing resource quantities (stocks) and movement rates (flows). Essential for understanding and communicating how an economy works before building it.
When resources accumulate faster than they are consumed, everything feels cheap and rewards lose meaning. The most common economy problem in MMOs and live-service games.
Free currency keeps free players engaged; premium currency monetizes convenience or cosmetics. The balance must ensure neither group feels the game is unfair to them.

learning_objectives: Design a progression system that maintains player engagement across a full game arc
Model difficulty curves mathematically and identify sawtooth, staircase, and smooth ramp patterns
Apply gating mechanics (skill, resource, narrative, time) to control content access
Identify and mitigate power creep in long-running games with progression systems
readings: Game Balance by Schreiber & Brathwaite, Ch. 8-9
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 15
Level Up! by Scott Rogers, Ch. 12 (Difficulty and Progression)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Progression is the long arc of a player's journey through a game. A well-designed progression system keeps the player engaged from the first minute to the last hour by continuously offering new content, new challenges, and new capabilities at a pace that matches the player's growing skill.

Progression operates on multiple timescales simultaneously. In a single play session, the player may gain a level, unlock a new ability, and complete a quest. Across sessions, they advance through the story, accumulate resources, and build toward long-term goals. Across the entire game, they transform from a novice into a master, with the game's mechanical complexity growing to match their expanding skill set. The designer must manage all three timescales to prevent the experience from feeling either overwhelming or stale.

Difficulty curves are the designer's primary tool for pacing challenge. A sawtooth curve alternates between rising tension and brief relief, creating a rhythm that prevents fatigue. A staircase curve uses flat plateaus with sharp jumps, creating distinct skill-check gates. A smooth ramp gradually increases difficulty with no relief, which works for short games but causes fatigue in long ones. The shape of the curve determines the emotional rhythm of the experience, and mismatched curves are a leading cause of player drop-off.
key_concepts: Linear progression: content experienced in a fixed order; tight authorial control but low replayability (Call of Duty campaigns)
Open progression: player chooses which content to tackle; high freedom but risk of sequence-breaking (Breath of the Wild)
Experience curves: linear XP requirements (steady), exponential XP requirements (accelerating grind), S-curve (fast start, slow middle, fast end)
Difficulty curves: sawtooth (rising tension with relief valleys), staircase (plateaus with sharp gates), smooth ramp (continuous increase)
Gating types: skill gates (require mastery), resource gates (require accumulation), narrative gates (require story progress), time gates (require real-world time)
Power creep: the gradual increase in power level of new content/items that invalidates older content and destabilizes balance over time
Catch-up mechanics: systems that help returning or new players reach current content without playing through obsolete progression (level boosts, accelerated XP)
Prestige/rebirth systems: resetting progression for cosmetic rewards or bonuses, extending the progression loop indefinitely
The mid-game content valley: the point where tutorial novelty has worn off but endgame rewards are still distant; the most common drop-off point
Pacing metrics: tracking player advancement rate, session length, and return rate to identify progression problems empirically
lecture_notes.demonstration: Graphing and comparing XP curves from Diablo II (exponential), Dark Souls (staircase difficulty), and Celeste (sawtooth with accessibility assists), then designing a progression system for a hypothetical mobile RPG using spreadsheet modeling.
setup_requirements: Graphing software or graph paper for curve visualization
Spreadsheet for XP curve modeling
Design journal

review.value: Sawtooth (tension/relief rhythm), staircase (plateaus with gates), smooth ramp (continuous increase). Shape determines emotional pacing; wrong shape causes drop-off.
New content being stronger than old content, gradually invalidating earlier designs. The primary long-term balance threat in games with progression systems.
The dangerous point where novelty has faded but endgame rewards are distant. Addressed by introducing new mechanics, story beats, or social features at this stage.
Controlling access to content through skill, resources, narrative, or time requirements. Good gating feels like earning access; bad gating feels like artificial delay.

learning_objectives: Define emergent gameplay and distinguish it from scripted/authored content
Identify the system properties that enable meaningful emergence (orthogonal mechanics, consistent rules, compositional interactions)
Design a minimal rule set that produces emergent behavior and playtest it
Evaluate the designer's control dilemma: balancing emergence with intent
readings: Rules of Play by Salen & Zimmerman, Ch. 22
Emergent Gameplay in Game Design by Joris Dormans (GDC presentation transcript)
Thinking in Systems by Donella Meadows, Ch. 3-4

lecture_notes.introduction:
The most memorable moments in games are often ones the designer never explicitly created. A desperate improvised solution using unexpected item combinations in Breath of the Wild. A cascading city-wide disaster in SimCity triggered by a single misplaced road. A player-organized heist in EVE Online involving months of social manipulation. These are examples of emergent gameplay: complex, unscripted behaviors that arise from the interaction of simple, well-defined systems.

Emergence is not randomness. Random events are unpredictable but meaningless; they happen to the player. Emergent events are unpredictable but meaningful; they happen because of the player's actions interacting with the game's systems in ways the designer enabled but did not specifically author. The key to designing for emergence is building systems with orthogonal mechanics: independent systems that interact in meaningful ways, multiplying the possibility space beyond what any single system provides.

The designer's dilemma with emergence is control. The more emergent a game is, the less the designer can predict or control specific player experiences. Some emergence is delightful (creative puzzle solutions). Some is destructive (exploit strategies that break the game). The challenge is building systems that produce the former while minimizing the latter, which requires deep understanding of how simple rules interact to produce complex outcomes.
key_concepts: Emergence: complex, unscripted behavior arising from the interaction of simple, well-defined rules rather than from explicit designer authorship
Designed emergence vs. accidental emergence: systems intentionally built to produce varied outcomes vs. unintended interactions (bugs, exploits)
Orthogonal mechanics: independent systems that interact meaningfully (Breath of the Wild's fire + wind + metal + electricity creating hundreds of combinations from four elements)
Compositional interactions: when system outputs can become inputs for other systems, creating chains (fire spreads to grass, grass fire creates updraft, updraft carries bombs)
The possibility space: the total set of game states reachable through player action; emergence exponentially expands this space
Emergent narrative: player-generated stories from system interactions (Dwarf Fortress, RimWorld, Crusader Kings)
The control problem: more emergence means less designer control; the challenge is enabling delightful surprises while preventing destructive exploits
Conway's Game of Life: the canonical example of emergence from minimal rules (4 rules produce galaxies, oscillators, gliders, and infinite complexity)
Designing for emergence: provide a few simple, consistent rules; make systems interact; let players discover combinations; patch exploits that break intent
Testing emergent systems: exhaustive testing is impossible, so focus on identifying system interaction boundaries and stress-testing edge cases
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running Conway's Game of Life as a pure emergence demonstration, then analyzing how Breath of the Wild's elemental interaction system (fire, ice, electricity, magnetism, wind) creates emergent puzzle solutions from five simple rules.
setup_requirements: Conway's Game of Life simulator (online or local)
Design journal for rule set design exercise
Index cards for tabletop emergence prototyping

review.value: Complex, unscripted behavior arising from simple system interactions. Not randomness; emergence is meaningful because it results from player actions within designed systems.
Independent systems that interact meaningfully. N orthogonal systems produce exponentially more combinations than N isolated systems, vastly expanding the possibility space.
More emergence means less designer control over specific outcomes. The art is enabling delightful surprises while preventing game-breaking exploits.
When the output of one system becomes the input of another, creating chains of cause and effect the designer never explicitly scripted.

learning_objectives: Analyze the structural differences between competitive, cooperative, and mixed-motive game designs
Apply game theory concepts (prisoner's dilemma, Nash equilibrium, Pareto optimality) to multiplayer design
Design mechanics that incentivize cooperation without eliminating competition
Evaluate the social dynamics that emerge from different multiplayer structures
readings: Characteristics of Games by Elias, Garfield & Gutschera, Ch. 9-11
Game Theory: A Very Short Introduction by Ken Binmore, Ch. 1-3
A Theory of Fun by Raph Koster, Ch. 7-8 (social games)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Multiplayer games are fundamentally different from single-player games because another human being is the most complex, unpredictable, and interesting system any designer can incorporate. The moment two or more players interact, game theory, the mathematical study of strategic decision-making, becomes essential.

Competitive games pit players directly against each other, with one player's gain being another's loss (zero-sum). Cooperative games unite players against a shared challenge, where everyone wins or loses together. Mixed-motive games, arguably the most interesting category, create situations where players must simultaneously cooperate and compete, producing complex social dynamics like trust, betrayal, negotiation, and alliance formation.

The Prisoner's Dilemma is the foundational concept for understanding these dynamics. Two players each choose to cooperate or defect. If both cooperate, both get a good outcome. If one defects while the other cooperates, the defector gets the best outcome and the cooperator gets the worst. If both defect, both get a mediocre outcome. The tension between individual incentive (defect) and collective benefit (cooperate) drives some of the most compelling multiplayer experiences in gaming.
key_concepts: Zero-sum games: one player's gain equals another's loss; strictly competitive (Chess, Tennis, fighting games)
Positive-sum games: cooperation can create value that benefits all players (trade in Settlers of Catan, team objectives in MMOs)
The Prisoner's Dilemma: the tension between individual incentive to defect and collective benefit of cooperation
Nash equilibrium: the strategy profile where no player can improve their outcome by unilaterally changing strategy; the stable point of competitive systems
Pareto optimality: an outcome where no player can be made better off without making another worse off; the 'efficient' outcome
Tit-for-tat: the simplest effective strategy in iterated Prisoner's Dilemma; cooperate first, then mirror the opponent's previous action
Free rider problem: in cooperative games, the incentive for individuals to contribute less than their share while benefiting from others' effort
Team composition and role design: creating mechanical interdependence so cooperation feels necessary rather than optional
Kingmaker problem: when an eliminated player's last action determines who wins among remaining players; a common competitive design flaw
Social contracts: unwritten rules that emerge in player communities (no camping, no griefing, trade etiquette) and the designer's role in supporting them
lecture_notes.demonstration: Playing an iterated Prisoner's Dilemma tournament in class, tracking strategies and outcomes over 20 rounds, then analyzing which strategies dominated and why. Connecting results to multiplayer game design decisions.
setup_requirements: Score tracking spreadsheet for tournament
Decision cards (cooperate/defect) for each student pair
Game theory reference sheet with payoff matrices (provided)

review.value: Individual incentive favors defection; collective incentive favors cooperation. This tension drives the most interesting multiplayer dynamics and is the foundation of mixed-motive game design.

The strategy profile where no player can improve by changing strategy alone. Finding the Nash equilibrium reveals what rational players will actually do, which may differ from what the designer wants them to do.

In cooperative games, individuals are incentivized to contribute less while benefiting from others' work. Addressed through individual accountability mechanics, role interdependence, or public contribution tracking.
Games where players must simultaneously cooperate and compete produce the richest social dynamics: trust, negotiation, betrayal, alliance formation, and reputation management.

learning_objectives: Design a game with asymmetric roles where each role has a fundamentally different play experience
Balance asymmetric roles using the principle of different but equivalent agency
Analyze how information asymmetry creates tension and strategic depth
Evaluate the onboarding challenges of asymmetric designs and propose solutions
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 7
Root: A Game of Woodland Might and Right (Designer Diary by Cole Wehrle, available online)
Dead by Daylight Design Postmortem (GDC talk on 1-vs-4 asymmetry)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Asymmetric design is one of the most powerful and challenging techniques in game design. Instead of giving all players the same options and the same experience, asymmetric games give different players fundamentally different roles, abilities, goals, or information. The result is a game where each player's experience is unique, replay value is multiplied by the number of roles, and every game feels different depending on your perspective.

The board game Root exemplifies ambitious asymmetric design. One player leads a woodland army controlling territory through military might. Another plays a guerrilla insurgency winning through hit-and-run tactics and public sympathy. A third is a wandering adventurer completing quests for points. A fourth is an industrial tycoon building trading posts. Each faction has completely different mechanics, different victory conditions, and different strategic considerations. Yet the game is competitive and balanced because each faction's strengths are checked by another faction's abilities.

The greatest challenge of asymmetric design is balance. When all players have the same options, balance means making those options equivalent. When players have completely different options, balance means making radically different experiences feel equally fair and equally fun. This requires extensive playtesting, mathematical modeling, and a willingness to make fundamental changes when data reveals imbalance.
key_concepts: Types of asymmetry: different starting positions, different abilities, different goals, different information, different resources, different game phases
Information asymmetry: one player knows something others do not (hidden role games, fog of war, asymmetric visibility)
Mechanical asymmetry: players interact with fundamentally different game systems (1-vs-many games like Dead by Daylight, MOBA role specialization)
Goal asymmetry: players pursue different win conditions (one player wins by conquest, another by economic dominance, another by cultural influence)
The 'different but equivalent' principle: each role should offer different strategic experiences while providing comparable agency and fun
Balancing asymmetric games: win-rate tracking per role, matchup matrices, adjusting strengths/weaknesses based on data rather than intuition
Onboarding challenge: asymmetric games require players to learn multiple rule sets (their own plus enough about others to strategize against them)
Asymmetry in cooperative games: tank/healer/DPS roles in MMOs; each player contributes differently to the shared goal
The spectacle of asymmetry: different roles create dramatic moments when their unique abilities collide in unexpected ways
Case studies: Root (4-faction asymmetry), Dead by Daylight (1v4), Netrunner (Corp vs. Runner), StarCraft (3-race asymmetry)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Playing a round of a simple asymmetric card game (provided) where one player is the 'builder' constructing structures and the other is the 'saboteur' trying to prevent it, then analyzing what makes the experience feel balanced or unbalanced from each perspective.
setup_requirements: Asymmetric card game kit (provided in class)
Feedback forms for each role's experience
Design journal for asymmetry design exercise

review.value: Asymmetric roles must provide different strategic experiences while ensuring comparable agency, fun, and winning probability. Different does not mean unequal.
When one player knows something others do not, it creates tension, deduction, and bluffing dynamics. The hidden information must be actionable for both the informed and uninformed player.
Asymmetric games require learning your own rules plus understanding opponents' capabilities. Addressed through gradual role introduction, reference cards, and spectating before playing.
In asymmetric games, balance is evaluated by matchup (Role A vs. Role B win rate) rather than overall (each role's global win rate), because matchup imbalance can be hidden by averaging.

learning_objectives: Design mechanics that encourage meaningful social interaction between players
Analyze how communication channels (chat, voice, ping, emote) shape social dynamics
Evaluate the role of trust, reputation, and social contracts in multiplayer communities
Propose design solutions for common social problems: toxicity, griefing, and free-riding
readings: Designing Virtual Worlds by Richard Bartle, Ch. 6-7
Community Building on the Web by Amy Jo Kim, Ch. 3-5
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 20 (Communities)

lecture_notes.introduction:
The most complex system in any multiplayer game is not the physics engine or the economy. It is the social system, the web of relationships, expectations, norms, and emotions that emerge when human beings interact within a shared rule structure. Every mechanical decision in a multiplayer game is simultaneously a social design decision, because mechanics shape how players treat each other.

Consider a simple example: in a cooperative game, do you share loot automatically or let players pick it up individually? Automatic sharing removes conflict but also removes generosity. Individual pickup creates opportunities for both generosity (giving a potion to a teammate who needs it) and selfishness (hoarding rare items). The mechanical choice determines whether the social dynamic is collaborative or competitive, trusting or suspicious.

Social mechanics are mechanics that explicitly mediate player-to-player interaction: trading systems, guild structures, voting mechanisms, communication channels, reputation systems, reporting tools. These are among the hardest mechanics to design because human behavior is vastly more complex and unpredictable than any algorithmic system. A well-designed social mechanic can create lasting communities, deep friendships, and memorable experiences. A poorly designed one can enable harassment, exploitation, and toxic environments that drive players away.
key_concepts: Communication channels: text chat, voice chat, ping systems, emotes, pre-set messages; each channel enables different social dynamics and different toxicity vectors
Trust mechanics: systems that require players to rely on each other (trading, cooperative puzzles, healing roles, shared resources)
Reputation systems: tracking player behavior over time and making it visible (honor scores, karma, endorsements, report counts)
Social contracts: emergent norms that players enforce informally (don't steal kills, rotate objectives, follow meta strategies)
Toxicity mitigation: mute tools, reporting systems, matchmaking based on behavior, positive reinforcement for good behavior, chat filters
Griefing: intentionally disrupting other players' experience; prevented through mechanics that limit destructive actions, not just punishment after the fact
Gift economies: systems where value flows through voluntary giving rather than explicit trading (gifting items, sharing crafting results)
Guild and clan mechanics: formal social structures with roles, permissions, shared resources, and collective goals that create belonging
Social proof and visibility: making positive contributions visible (leaderboards for helpfulness, badges for mentoring) encourages prosocial behavior through social pressure
The observer effect: knowing your behavior is visible changes how you behave; transparency tools shape social norms more effectively than punishment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the social mechanics of three multiplayer games: Among Us (deception and trust), Deep Rock Galactic (cooperative communication), and League of Legends (competitive tension and toxicity), identifying which mechanics encourage positive interaction and which enable negative.
setup_requirements: Design journal for social mechanics analysis
Case study handouts for each game (provided)
Group discussion seating arrangement

review.value: Each communication channel (chat, voice, ping, emote) enables different social dynamics. Restricting channels reduces toxicity but also reduces positive interactions. The design must balance both.
Making behavior history visible changes how players act. Effective reputation systems track behavior over time, are resistant to gaming, and have meaningful consequences.
Mechanical design is social design. How loot is distributed, how teams are formed, how communication works, all shape whether the community is generous or toxic.
The best approach to griefing is mechanics that make destructive actions impossible or unprofitable, not punishing players after the damage is done.

learning_objectives: Describe common player modeling approaches (behavioral, statistical, machine-learning-based)
Design a simple adaptive system that responds to inferred player skill or preference
Evaluate the trade-offs between player autonomy and system-driven adaptation
Apply segmentation models (Bartle, Quantic Foundry) to inform adaptive design decisions
readings: AI for Games by Ian Millington, Ch. 14 (Player Modeling)
Quantic Foundry Gamer Motivation Model (online documentation)
Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment: An Exploration (GDC talk by Jenova Chen)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Player modeling is the practice of building a computational representation of the player's skill, preferences, behavior patterns, and emotional state, then using that model to adapt the game experience in real time. It is the bridge between understanding player psychology (which we covered in GD101) and implementing adaptive systems that respond to individual players.

The simplest form of player modeling is manual difficulty selection: the player tells the system their skill level by choosing Easy, Normal, or Hard. More sophisticated models observe player behavior and infer skill or preference without asking. Resident Evil 4's dynamic difficulty system tracks deaths, damage taken, and hit accuracy to silently adjust enemy aggressiveness and item drop rates. Left 4 Dead's AI Director monitors pacing and stress to decide when to spawn hordes and when to give players a breather.

The most advanced models use machine learning to cluster players into behavioral types and predict future behavior based on historical patterns. This enables personalized content recommendation, individualized difficulty adjustment, and targeted retention mechanics. However, these systems raise important questions about transparency, player autonomy, and the ethical use of behavioral data.
key_concepts: Behavioral telemetry: tracking what players do (time played, deaths, paths taken, items used) to build behavioral profiles
Skill modeling: estimating player ability from performance metrics (completion time, accuracy, deaths per level, resource efficiency)
Preference modeling: inferring what players enjoy from behavioral data (exploration time, combat frequency, story engagement, social interaction)
Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA): silently adjusting game parameters based on inferred skill to maintain the flow channel
The AI Director pattern: a system that controls pacing by adjusting intensity in real time (Left 4 Dead, Resident Evil 4)
Segmentation models: Bartle types, Quantic Foundry motivations, player lifecycle stages (newbie, regular, hardcore, at-risk, churned)
Personalized content: recommending activities, challenges, or items based on modeled preferences
Transparency vs. stealth: should the player know the system is adapting? Transparent systems build trust; stealth systems avoid gaming-the-system behavior
Ethical considerations: data privacy, consent, the line between adapting-for-the-player and manipulating-the-player
Model validation: how do you know your player model is accurate? A/B testing, prediction accuracy, and qualitative validation through interviews
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a simple DDA system on paper for a hypothetical action game: defining what metrics to track, what parameters to adjust, how aggressively to adapt, and where to set the transparency boundary. Then modeling two simulated player profiles and predicting how the system would respond to each.
setup_requirements: Design journal for DDA system design
Spreadsheet for modeling player behavior and system responses
Player profile templates (provided)

review.value: Silently adjusting game parameters based on observed player performance to maintain the flow channel. Effective when invisible; breaks immersion when the player notices the system gaming them.
A real-time pacing system that controls intensity. Monitors stress metrics and adjusts encounter frequency, enemy strength, and resource availability to maintain dramatic rhythm.
Transparent adaptation builds trust but lets players game the system. Stealth adaptation feels more natural but undermines player autonomy if discovered.
Using player models to improve the experience is good design. Using them to manipulate spending or extend play sessions beyond the player's interest is exploitation.

learning_objectives: Define the metagame and distinguish it from core gameplay in competitive and cooperative contexts
Analyze how metagames form, evolve, and are disrupted in live-service games
Design outer-loop systems (seasonal content, battle passes, ranked progression) that sustain long-term engagement
Evaluate the health of a metagame using diversity, staleness, and accessibility metrics
readings: Characteristics of Games by Elias, Garfield & Gutschera, Ch. 12-13
Playing to Win by David Sirlin, Ch. 1-3
The Metagame: Understanding Competitive Balance in Live Games (Riot Games developer blog)

lecture_notes.introduction:
The metagame, often shortened to 'meta,' refers to the layer of strategy and engagement that sits above the core gameplay. In competitive games, the meta is the evolving consensus about which strategies, characters, or compositions are strongest at any given time. In broader terms, it encompasses all the systems and content that keep players engaged between and beyond individual play sessions: ranked ladders, seasonal events, battle passes, collection mechanics, and community-driven strategy evolution.

The competitive metagame is a living organism. When a new patch buffs a character, players flock to that character. When enough players use the same character, counter-strategies emerge. When counter-strategies become dominant, the original character's win rate drops, and the meta shifts again. This cycle of dominance, counter-play, and adaptation is the sign of a healthy metagame. An unhealthy meta is one where the cycle stalls: a single strategy remains dominant for too long, and no counter-play emerges.

The outer-loop metagame is equally important for long-term engagement. A player may love the core gameplay of a competitive shooter, but after hundreds of hours, they need additional motivation: ranked progression, seasonal rewards, cosmetic collections, battle pass challenges, or community events. These outer-loop systems must add motivation without distorting the core gameplay. A battle pass challenge that says 'get 50 headshots with the worst weapon' can ruin competitive matches by incentivizing suboptimal play.
key_concepts: Competitive metagame: the evolving consensus about optimal strategies in a game's competitive community (tier lists, meta picks, standard builds)
Meta evolution cycle: dominance, counter-play, adaptation, new dominance; the healthy competitive ecosystem
Meta staleness: when a single strategy remains dominant too long, reducing variety and player engagement; addressed through patches, new content, or meta shifts
Outer-loop systems: ranked progression, seasonal content, battle passes, collection mechanics, prestige systems that sustain engagement beyond core gameplay
Battle pass design: linear progression with free and premium tracks, challenges that encourage varied gameplay, time-limited rewards that drive regular play
Seasonal content cycles: regular cadence of new content, balance changes, and events that create a sense of ongoing novelty and community rhythm
Meta diversity metrics: measuring how many viable strategies exist at any given time; high diversity = healthy meta, low diversity = stale meta
Meta accessibility: whether new players can engage with the current meta or are locked out by knowledge barriers, collection requirements, or skill gaps
The Sirlin model: in a deep game, losing should always teach you something; the meta should reward learning and adaptation, not just having the strongest options
Community meta management: developer communication about balance intent, patch notes culture, community content creators as meta amplifiers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the current metagame of a popular competitive game (e.g., a MOBA or card game) by examining tier lists, win rates, pick rates, and ban rates to evaluate meta health, diversity, and accessibility.
setup_requirements: Access to a competitive game's public statistics (e.g., op.gg, HSReplay, Mobalytics)
Spreadsheet for meta diversity analysis
Design journal for meta design exercise

review.value: Dominance breeds counter-play, which breeds new dominance. This cycle keeps the competitive experience fresh. If the cycle stalls, the meta is stale.
Ranked progression, seasonal content, and battle passes sustain long-term engagement beyond core gameplay. Must add motivation without distorting core gameplay.
Viable strategy count, pick rate distribution, win rate variance, and ban rate patterns. A healthy meta has many viable strategies with no extreme outliers.
Challenges should encourage gameplay variety, not distort competitive play. Time requirements should be achievable for moderate players. Free track must feel valuable.

class_schedule.topic: Systems Integration and Playtesting Complex Mechanics
learning_objectives: Integrate multiple mechanical systems into a cohesive game design with controlled interactions
Develop a playtesting plan for complex systems that isolates individual mechanics before testing interactions
Apply stress-testing techniques to find degenerate strategies and exploit paths in interconnected systems
Document system interactions formally using interaction matrices and dependency diagrams
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 10-11
Game Balance by Schreiber & Brathwaite, Ch. 11-12
Postmortem: What Went Wrong in Game X (selected developer postmortem)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Individual mechanics are relatively easy to design and balance in isolation. The hard part is making them work together. A combat system that is beautifully balanced on its own can break completely when connected to an economy system that provides unlimited resources. A progression system that paces perfectly in single-player can create insurmountable gaps in multiplayer. Systems integration is where most game design problems originate and where the most difficult design work happens.

The challenge is combinatorial. If your game has 5 mechanical systems that each interact with every other system, you have 10 two-way interactions to manage. Add a 6th system and you have 15 interactions. Add a 7th and you have 21. Each interaction is a potential source of degenerate strategies, exploit paths, and unintended behaviors. You cannot test every possible combination, so you need systematic approaches to identify the most dangerous interactions and test them first.

Today we focus on the practical skills of systems integration: how to map interactions formally, how to design playtesting plans that isolate and then combine systems, how to stress-test for degenerate strategies, and how to document system dependencies so that changing one system does not accidentally break another.
key_concepts: Interaction matrices: a grid mapping which systems interact with which others, with notes on the nature of each interaction
Dependency diagrams: directed graphs showing which systems depend on which others, revealing cascade paths when changes propagate
Isolation testing: testing each system independently to verify it works correctly before testing interactions
Integration testing: testing two systems together, then three, building up to the full game incrementally
Stress testing: deliberately trying to break the game by maximizing or minimizing specific resources, abilities, or strategies
Degenerate strategies: player behaviors that technically follow the rules but exploit system interactions to bypass intended gameplay (infinite resource loops, invincibility combos)
The exploit spectrum: from creative use of mechanics (celebrated) to game-breaking exploits (patched), with a gray zone in between
Change impact analysis: before modifying any system, tracing the change through the dependency diagram to predict which other systems will be affected
Version-controlled balance: tracking every numerical change with its date, rationale, and observed impact, creating an audit trail for balance decisions

The 'one change at a time' principle: when debugging system interactions, change one variable at a time and test; multiple simultaneous changes make it impossible to identify which change caused the effect
lecture_notes.demonstration: Group exercise where teams combine three mechanical systems (combat, economy, progression) in a paper prototype, play for 15 minutes, then identify the most degenerate strategy each team discovered. Discussion of how to fix each exploit without breaking the systems' individual design.
setup_requirements: Paper prototype supplies (cards, dice, tokens, markers)
Interaction matrix template (provided)
Dependency diagram template (provided)
Design journal for integration notes

review.value: A grid showing which systems interact with which others. Reveals the number and nature of interactions, highlighting the most complex and dangerous connection points.
Test each system alone first, then combine systems incrementally. This approach localizes bugs to specific interactions rather than searching the entire system.
Player behaviors that exploit system interactions to bypass intended gameplay. Found through stress testing and adversarial playtesting by experienced players trying to break the game.
When debugging complex system interactions, change one variable and test. Multiple simultaneous changes make root cause identification impossible.

learning_objectives: Present a complete mechanical system design with formal documentation and quantitative analysis
Demonstrate the ability to diagnose and fix balance problems using course concepts
Receive and integrate peer feedback on systems design constructively
Articulate a personal philosophy of mechanical design informed by the semester's work
readings: Review all course materials and design journal entries
Prepare presentation materials for final project

lecture_notes.introduction:
This final session is devoted to presenting your semester-long projects: original mechanical system designs with formal analysis, balance modeling, and playtesting results. Each team will present their system, walk us through the design decisions and the data that informed them, and demonstrate the playable prototype.

Your presentation should tell a story: what problem were you trying to solve, what systems did you design, what happened when players interacted with those systems, what broke, what you fixed, and what the final result demonstrates about mechanical design. I am most interested in your analytical process: how you diagnosed problems, how you used data to inform changes, and how you balanced competing design goals.

After presentations, we will conduct a course retrospective. We have covered mechanics taxonomy, feedback loops, balance theory, reward systems, probability, economics, progression, emergence, competitive and cooperative design, asymmetry, social mechanics, player modeling, metagame design, and systems integration. That is a comprehensive toolkit for any game designer. The question is: how will you use it? Your personal design philosophy is the answer.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: problem statement, system design, mechanical specification, playtesting methodology, data analysis, iterations, final result, lessons learned
Data-driven argumentation: supporting design decisions with playtest data, not just intuition or preference
Balance evidence: showing win rates, strategy distribution, completion rates, or other quantitative metrics that demonstrate the system works as intended
Honest post-mortem: acknowledging what did not work is as valuable as celebrating what did; the best designers learn more from failures
Peer feedback protocol: specific observations, actionable suggestions, questions for the designer to consider
Personal design philosophy: the principles and priorities that guide your mechanical design decisions, informed by the course's frameworks and your practical experience
Course synthesis: connecting all 15 weeks of content into a unified understanding of how game systems work
Next steps: advanced courses, game jams, industry applications, independent projects, portfolio development
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student team presentations (10-12 minutes each) followed by 5 minutes of peer and instructor Q&A per team.
setup_requirements: Final project documentation (printed and digital)
Presentation slides or visual aids
Playable prototype (physical or digital)
Balance data and analysis spreadsheets

review.value: The hallmark of professional systems design is supporting decisions with evidence. Intuition starts the process; data finishes it.
Acknowledging failures demonstrates maturity and analytical depth. What you learned from what broke is often more valuable than what worked perfectly.

Mechanics taxonomy, feedback loops, balance, rewards, probability, economics, progression, emergence, competition/cooperation, asymmetry, social design, player modeling, metagame, and integration. A complete systems design toolkit.
Your own set of principles about what matters most in mechanical design, developed through the course's frameworks and your hands-on experience building, testing, and iterating.

quizzes.topic: Core Mechanics, Feedback Loops, and Game Balance

questions.question: Monopoly is often criticized for having a strong positive feedback loop. Identify the specific mechanic that creates this loop, explain why it makes the game less fun, and propose one rule modification that introduces a negative feedback loop to counterbalance it. Your modification must be implementable with the existing game components.
questions.answer: The mechanic is property ownership combined with rent: owning more properties generates more rent income, which allows buying more properties, which generates even more rent. This is a positive feedback loop because advantage compounds with each circuit of the board. It makes the game less fun because the outcome becomes effectively decided long before the game officially ends, trapping losing players in a hopeless, drawn-out experience. Modification: progressive maintenance costs. At the start of each turn, a player pays $50 per property owned beyond their third property. This drains resources from the leader proportionally to their advantage, giving trailing players more time to acquire properties and reducing the runaway leader effect. It is implementable with existing components (money from the bank).

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the property-rent positive feedback mechanic with the compounding mechanism
Explains why the loop degrades the experience (decided outcome, trapped losers)
Proposed modification creates a genuine negative feedback loop that scales with advantage

questions.question: Explain the difference between transitive and intransitive balance relationships. For each, give a specific example from a published game and explain when each relationship type is the better design choice.
questions.answer: Transitive balance creates a strict hierarchy: A is always better than B, B is always better than C. Example: in most RPGs, equipment tiers form a transitive chain (Iron Sword < Steel Sword < Mythril Sword). Transitive relationships are the better choice for progression systems where the designer wants to reward investment and create a satisfying sense of growth. Intransitive balance creates circular dominance: A beats B, B beats C, C beats A, with no universally dominant option. Example: Pokemon's type system (Water beats Fire, Fire beats Grass, Grass beats Water). Intransitive relationships are the better choice for competitive multiplayer where the designer wants to prevent dominant strategies and encourage adaptive counter-play.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of both transitive and intransitive with clear structural distinction
Accurate examples from real games for both types
Explains when each is the better design choice with reasoning tied to design goals

questions.question: A card game has 50 different cards. Playtest data shows that experienced players always build decks around the same 8 cards and ignore the other 42. Using balance concepts from the course, what does this pattern indicate, and what are two different approaches to fixing it?
questions.answer: This pattern indicates that those 8 cards are dominant: their power-to-cost ratio is so much higher than the other 42 that including any other card weakens the deck. The 42 unused cards are functionally non-existent, reducing the game's meaningful decision space from 50 to 8. Fix 1 (Buff the weak): Increase the power of underused cards to make them competitive with the top 8, either by reducing their cost or increasing their effects. This expands viable deck options without taking anything away from players. Fix 2 (Nerf the strong): Reduce the power of the dominant 8 cards so that their advantage over alternatives is smaller. This is faster to implement and directly addresses the outliers, but risks angering players who enjoy those cards. Ideally, combine both: slight nerfs to extreme outliers and targeted buffs to the most promising underused cards.

rubric.key: Correctly diagnoses dominant strategy / cost-curve outlier problem
Two distinct, specific approaches to fixing the balance problem
Acknowledges trade-offs of each approach

questions.question: A mobile game uses a gacha system: 2% chance of top-tier character (worth 100 value units), 18% chance of mid-tier (worth 20 units), 80% chance of low-tier (worth 1 unit). Calculate the expected value of a single pull. Then explain, using behavioral psychology, why players continue pulling despite the low expected value.
questions.answer: Expected value = (0.02 x 100) + (0.18 x 20) + (0.80 x 1) = 2 + 3.6 + 0.8 = 6.4 value units per pull. Players continue despite the low average because variable-ratio reinforcement creates the strongest habit formation. The unpredictable timing of a rare, high-value reward produces intermittent reinforcement, which behavioral psychology shows generates the most persistent response patterns and highest resistance to extinction. Additionally, near-miss effects (getting a mid-tier when hoping for top-tier) sustain perceived closeness to a big win, and loss aversion makes players reluctant to stop after investing heavily without getting the desired result (sunk cost fallacy).

rubric.key: Correct expected value calculation showing work (6.4 value units)
References variable-ratio reinforcement or intermittent reinforcement with correct description
Explains at least one additional psychological mechanism (near-miss, sunk cost, loss aversion)

questions.question: Explain the difference between input randomness and output randomness. For each type, name a published game that uses it prominently and explain why that type of randomness is appropriate for that game's design goals.
questions.answer: Input randomness occurs before the player makes a decision: the random element is revealed, and the player chooses how to respond with full information. Example: Slay the Spire presents randomly drawn cards at the start of each turn. The player sees the hand and makes strategic decisions about which cards to play. This is appropriate because the game's design goal is strategic adaptation: each run is different, and the skill is in making the best decision with what you are given. Output randomness occurs after the player makes a decision: the player commits to an action, and randomness determines the outcome. Example: XCOM's combat where a 75% hit chance still misses 1 in 4 times. This is appropriate because the game's design goal is simulating the chaos of tactical warfare, where even good plans can go wrong, creating dramatic tension and requiring contingency planning.

rubric.key: Correct definitions distinguishing when the randomness resolves relative to the player's decision
Appropriate game examples for each type with the random mechanic identified
Explains why each randomness type matches the game's specific design goals

questions.question: A strategy game has a 10% critical hit chance. In testing, players report that critical hits 'never happen' even though telemetry confirms they occur at exactly 10%. Using concepts from the course, explain this perception gap and propose one mechanical solution that maintains the 10% average rate while making it feel more fair.
questions.answer: The perception gap exists because of two cognitive biases. First, players overestimate the frequency of rare events in expectation (they expect 10% to feel common) but underestimate it in memory (they forget the crits that happened and remember the long droughts). Second, true random 10% chance produces streak patterns, including long drought stretches, that feel unfair even though they are mathematically expected. A streak of 20 non-crits has a 12% chance of occurring in any 20-attack sequence, which is common enough to happen regularly. Solution: Implement a pseudo-random distribution (PRD) where the actual crit chance starts below 10% and increases with each non-crit, resetting on a crit. The average rate remains 10%, but long droughts become extremely unlikely. This smooths the distribution, making the 10% rate feel like 10% instead of feeling like 3%.

rubric.key: Identifies the perception bias (overestimation of rare events, negativity bias toward droughts)
Explains why true random produces feel-bad streaks despite correct average
Proposes PRD or pity timer solution that maintains the 10% average while reducing variance

questions.question: An MMO's economy is experiencing severe inflation: gold has become nearly worthless, and item prices have risen by 500% over six months. Identify two likely causes using economic concepts from the course, and propose one source adjustment and one sink addition that would stabilize the economy.
questions.answer: Cause 1: Gold sources (quest rewards, mob drops, daily rewards) are generating more gold than sinks are removing, creating a net positive gold flow into the economy that accumulates over time. Cause 2: As players reach endgame, they have fewer meaningful purchases (sinks), so gold piles up with nowhere to go, while sources continue generating at the same rate. Source adjustment: Reduce gold rewards for repeatable content (daily quests, mob drops) by 30-40%, targeting the highest-volume sources first. This reduces the inflow rate without affecting one-time story rewards that feel important. Sink addition: Introduce a gold-cost housing system with weekly maintenance fees proportional to house size. This creates a recurring sink that scales with player wealth (wealthier players buy bigger houses with higher maintenance), removing gold proportionally to how much has accumulated.

rubric.key: Two distinct causes that correctly apply source/sink imbalance concepts
Source adjustment targets the right type of source with specific reasoning
Sink addition is recurring, scales appropriately, and provides player value in exchange for gold removal

questions.question: Describe the 'sawtooth' difficulty curve pattern. Explain why it is more effective than a smooth ramp for maintaining engagement over long play sessions, and name a published game that uses it well.
questions.answer: A sawtooth difficulty curve alternates between periods of rising challenge (the incline of each tooth) and brief drops in intensity (the valley between teeth). Each peak is slightly higher than the previous one, so overall difficulty trends upward while providing regular relief. This is more effective than a smooth ramp because the rising sections push players toward the edge of their skill (creating flow and accomplishment) while the valleys provide relief that prevents fatigue and frustration. The valleys also serve as consolidation periods where players practice new skills in lower-pressure contexts before the next escalation. This mirrors natural attention and energy cycles. A smooth ramp provides no relief, causing cumulative fatigue that leads to quitting. Example: Celeste uses a sawtooth pattern beautifully. Each chapter introduces a new mechanic with escalating difficulty, but safe rooms with checkpoints appear regularly between challenging sections, and new chapters often start with gentle reintroduction of mechanics.

rubric.key: Accurately describes the sawtooth shape with peaks, valleys, and overall upward trend
Explains why tension-relief cycling beats continuous escalation with specific reasoning
Names a real game and explains how it uses the sawtooth pattern

questions.question: Define emergent gameplay and explain the difference between designed emergence and accidental emergence. Give one example of each from a published game.
questions.answer: Emergent gameplay is complex, unscripted behavior that arises from the interaction of simple, well-defined game systems rather than from explicit designer authorship. Designed emergence occurs when the designer intentionally creates systems whose interactions produce varied, unpredictable outcomes. Example: Breath of the Wild's physics system deliberately allows fire to spread to grass, grass fire to create updrafts, and updrafts to carry the player. These interactions are designed to be compositional, and the developers intended players to discover creative combinations. Accidental emergence occurs when system interactions produce behaviors the designer did not intend or foresee. Example: Rocket jumping in Quake, where players discovered they could aim the rocket launcher at the ground and jump simultaneously to propel themselves to otherwise unreachable heights. This was not designed as a movement mechanic but emerged from the interaction of projectile physics and knockback. It became celebrated and was preserved in subsequent games.

rubric.key: Clear definition of emergent gameplay distinguishing it from scripted content and randomness
Correct distinction between designed and accidental emergence with definitions
Accurate examples of each type from real games with explanation of why they qualify

quizzes.topic: Competitive/Cooperative Design, Asymmetry, and Social Mechanics
