questions.question: Explain the Prisoner's Dilemma and describe how it applies to a specific multiplayer game scenario. In your scenario, identify what 'cooperate' and 'defect' mean in game terms, and explain what the Nash equilibrium is.
questions.answer: The Prisoner's Dilemma is a game theory scenario where two players each choose to cooperate or defect. Mutual cooperation gives both a good outcome. If one defects while the other cooperates, the defector gets the best outcome and the cooperator gets the worst. Mutual defection gives both a mediocre outcome. Game scenario: In a battle royale's final circle, two solo players face a third, stronger player. 'Cooperate' means temporarily teaming up to eliminate the strong player. 'Defect' means attacking the other solo player instead. If both cooperate, they eliminate the threat and face each other in a fair 1v1. If one defects, they get an easy kill on the trusting player while avoiding the strong player. If both defect, they fight each other while the strong player picks off the survivor. The Nash equilibrium is mutual defection: each player's individual incentive is to defect regardless of what the other does, even though mutual cooperation would give both a better chance of winning.

rubric.key: Correct description of the Prisoner's Dilemma payoff structure
Specific game scenario with clear cooperate/defect actions and realistic payoffs
Correct identification and explanation of the Nash equilibrium in the scenario

questions.question: A 1-vs-4 asymmetric horror game (one powerful monster vs. four weaker survivors) has a balance problem: the monster wins 75% of matches at all skill levels. Propose two mechanical changes that would bring the win rate closer to 50% without fundamentally changing the asymmetric roles.
questions.answer: Change 1: Strengthen survivor cooperation incentives. Add a 'courage' mechanic where survivors near each other receive a damage bonus against the monster, rewarding grouping up rather than splitting. This amplifies the survivors' numerical advantage when they coordinate, which is the intended counter-play in 1-vs-4 design, without changing the monster's power in individual encounters. Change 2: Introduce monster power scaling based on survivor count. When the monster eliminates a survivor, remaining survivors get a scaling buff (increased speed, reduced ability cooldowns), making each subsequent kill harder. This creates a negative feedback loop that prevents the monster from snowballing an early advantage into a guaranteed win, while keeping early-game encounters terrifying. Neither change alters the fundamental asymmetry: the monster is still powerful and scary in 1v1, but the game now better rewards survivor teamwork and creates closer matches.

rubric.key: Two distinct mechanical changes that address the balance problem from different angles
Changes preserve the asymmetric identity (monster is powerful, survivors rely on teamwork)
At least one change uses a feedback mechanism concept from the course

questions.question: A competitive online game has a severe toxicity problem in its text chat. Propose two design-level solutions (not just moderation policies) that use mechanics to reduce toxic behavior. Explain the behavioral principle behind each.
questions.answer: Solution 1: Post-match honor system with matchmaking benefits. After each match, players can award honor points to teammates who communicated constructively. Players with high honor scores get matched with other high-honor players, creating a positive feedback loop where good behavior leads to better teammates, which encourages more good behavior. Behavioral principle: positive reinforcement (rewarding desired behavior) combined with social proof (visible honor badges encourage others to emulate). Solution 2: Replace unrestricted text chat with a contextual ping/emote system during high-tension moments (death, losing, falling behind). Text chat is available during downtime and post-match but restricted to pings during gameplay. Behavioral principle: environmental design / affordance restriction. Toxicity spikes during emotionally charged moments when players have access to unrestricted expression. Removing the channel during those moments prevents impulsive toxic messages without eliminating communication entirely.

rubric.key: Two distinct mechanical solutions that address toxicity through design, not just moderation
Each solution includes a specific behavioral principle with correct application
Solutions are practical and preserve necessary communication while reducing toxic vectors

quizzes.topic: Player Modeling, Metagame Design, and Systems Integration

questions.question: Describe Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA) and explain one advantage and one disadvantage of implementing it as a stealth system (invisible to the player) versus a transparent system (the player knows it is happening).
questions.answer: DDA is a system that adjusts game parameters (enemy health, damage, resource availability, puzzle complexity) in real time based on observed player performance to maintain the flow channel. Stealth DDA advantage: it preserves immersion because the player does not know the game is adapting. Achievements feel fully earned, and the experience feels consistent. Stealth DDA disadvantage: if the player discovers the system, it can feel patronizing or dishonest, undermining their sense of accomplishment ('I didn't really beat that boss; the game let me win'). Transparent DDA advantage: the player retains full autonomy because they know the system exists and can choose to engage with or override it. This builds trust and lets players who want a fixed challenge opt out. Transparent DDA disadvantage: players may game the system by intentionally performing poorly to trigger easier settings, then playing normally in the easier state, which undermines the system's purpose.

rubric.key: Correct definition of DDA with specific examples of what parameters are adjusted
Stealth advantage and disadvantage are distinct and demonstrate understanding of player autonomy and immersion
Transparent advantage and disadvantage are distinct and demonstrate understanding of trust and gaming behavior

questions.question: A competitive card game's meta has been dominated by the same deck archetype for three months. Using metagame concepts from the course, explain why this staleness is harmful and propose two different interventions (one that adds new content and one that changes existing content) to refresh the meta.
questions.answer: Staleness is harmful because it reduces meaningful choice (if one deck is dominant, choosing any other deck is objectively worse), decreases replay variety (every match feels the same), and drives player attrition (experienced players get bored, new players feel they must copy the dominant deck or lose). Intervention 1 (New content): Release a new card set that specifically introduces counter-mechanics to the dominant archetype. For example, if the dominant deck relies on swarming cheap creatures, introduce cards with powerful area-of-effect removal or bonuses for facing many small creatures. This creates a natural intransitive counter without removing the dominant deck from viability. Intervention 2 (Change existing): Nerf the two most critical cards in the dominant deck by increasing their cost or reducing their effect, just enough to bring the deck's win rate down from dominant to competitive. Simultaneously, buff two underused cards in the weakest archetypes to make them more viable. This rebalances the existing card pool and shifts the meta without requiring new purchases.

rubric.key: Explains why staleness is harmful with at least two specific consequences
New content intervention specifically counters the dominant archetype with intransitive design
Existing content intervention uses targeted adjustments rather than sledgehammer changes

questions.question: You are integrating a crafting system into an RPG that already has combat and progression systems. Using the interaction matrix concept, identify two specific interactions between crafting and the existing systems that could create degenerate strategies, and explain how you would prevent each.
questions.answer: Interaction 1: Crafting x Progression. If crafted items provide XP, players may discover that crafting the cheapest possible item repeatedly is more efficient than fighting enemies for XP, creating a degenerate 'craft-spam' leveling strategy that bypasses the combat system entirely. Prevention: Cap XP from crafting per level (e.g., crafting can contribute at most 20% of XP needed per level), and tie crafting XP to item tier rather than item count, so only challenging recipes yield meaningful XP. Interaction 2: Crafting x Combat (economy loop). If enemy drops provide crafting materials and crafted items make combat easier, a positive feedback loop forms: better crafted gear makes farming easier, faster farming produces more materials, more materials produce better gear. Prevention: Add material degradation (crafted weapons lose durability and need re-crafting) as a sink, and gate the highest-tier crafting recipes behind quest completion rather than just material accumulation, breaking the pure grind loop.

rubric.key: Two distinct interactions between crafting and existing systems correctly identified
Each degenerate strategy is plausible and clearly explained as a system interaction problem
Prevention strategies address the root interaction rather than just limiting the symptom

major_assignments.title: Mechanical Systems Analysis and Redesign
major_assignments.description: Select a published game with a known balance or pacing problem. Perform a formal systems analysis identifying all feedback loops, balance relationships, reward structures, and economic flows. Then propose and model a mechanical redesign that addresses the identified problems while preserving the game's core identity. Support your redesign with quantitative evidence from spreadsheet modeling.
objectives: Apply formal systems analysis tools (feedback loop diagrams, balance matrices, stock-and-flow diagrams) to a real game
Identify root causes of balance or pacing problems at the mechanical level, not just symptoms
Design and quantitatively model a mechanical solution using spreadsheet simulation
Communicate complex system interactions clearly through diagrams and professional writing
deliverables: Systems diagram of the original game's core mechanics, feedback loops, and reward structures using formal notation
Written analysis (5-7 pages) identifying specific balance or pacing problems with evidence from gameplay observation or community data
Proposed redesign with annotated systems diagram showing every change and its intended effect
Spreadsheet model simulating at least 20 rounds/turns/encounters before and after the redesign, demonstrating quantitative improvement

criteria: Accurate identification of core and secondary mechanics with correct categorization
Complete feedback loop diagram with correctly labeled positive and negative loops
Balance relationships mapped with transitive and intransitive labels where applicable
Clear identification of the root problem with supporting evidence, not just symptoms
Proposed changes target root causes identified in the analysis
Redesign preserves the game's core identity and appeal
Changes are specific, implementable, and numerically defined, not vague suggestions
Potential side effects of changes are acknowledged and addressed
Spreadsheet model correctly simulates game rounds with reasonable assumptions
Before and after comparison clearly demonstrates quantitative impact of changes
Assumptions are stated explicitly and justified
Variance and edge cases are considered, not just averages
Diagrams are clear, well-labeled, and use consistent formal notation
Written analysis is logically structured and persuasive
Technical terminology is used correctly throughout
Professional formatting and presentation quality
Thorough and accurate formal systems analysis of the chosen game
Insightful identification of root mechanical problems with evidence
Well-reasoned redesign supported by quantitative spreadsheet modeling
Clear, professional communication in both diagrams and writing

major_assignments.title: Emergent Mechanics Tabletop Game
major_assignments.description: Design and build an original tabletop game with no more than 5 rules that produces emergent gameplay. The game must be playable in 15-20 minutes and must demonstrate that simple, orthogonal mechanics can generate complex and surprising outcomes. Conduct at least 5 playtests, document emergent behaviors that arose during play, and write a reflection analyzing which system interactions produced emergence and why.
objectives: Design a minimal rule set that produces emergent complexity through orthogonal mechanics
Demonstrate understanding of how system interactions create behaviors that transcend individual rules
Observe, document, and categorize emergent behaviors across multiple playtest sessions
Evaluate the trade-off between designer control and emergent freedom based on practical experience
deliverables: Complete playable tabletop game with all physical components, ready for others to play without your presence
Rules document (one page maximum) with no more than 5 rules, clear enough for strangers to play
Playtest log documenting at least 5 sessions with specific notes on emergent behaviors observed in each
Reflection essay (4-5 pages) analyzing which system interactions produced emergence, what surprised you, and how your understanding of emergence evolved through the project

criteria: Five or fewer rules that are clear, unambiguous, and internally consistent
Rules create orthogonal interactions that enable genuine emergence, not just randomness
Game is playable and completable in 15-20 minutes with a clear end condition
Design demonstrates creativity, originality, and understanding of mechanical elegance
Documented emergent behaviors are genuine, non-trivial, and clearly distinct from designed behaviors
Emergence arises from system interactions, not from randomness alone or hidden complexity
Multiple distinct emergent strategies, outcomes, or narratives observed across sessions
Game produces meaningfully different experiences across play sessions
At least 5 sessions documented with specific, detailed observations
Notes distinguish between designed behavior and emergent behavior with reasoning
Quantitative data collected where relevant (win rates, strategy diversity, session duration variance)
Iterative changes noted and justified if any were made between sessions
Thoughtful analysis of which specific interactions produced emergence and why
Honest assessment of what worked, what fell flat, and what was surprising
Connections drawn to course concepts (orthogonal mechanics, possibility space, control problem)
Professional writing quality with clear structure and reflective depth
Elegant rule set that produces genuine emergent complexity from minimal components
Well-documented playtesting with rich observational data
Insightful reflection connecting practical experience to theoretical concepts
Polished, playable game that is genuinely enjoyable and replayable

major_assignments.title: Complete Mechanical System Design with Balance Analysis
major_assignments.description: Design a complete competitive game system with at least 3 asymmetric roles, an economic model, a progression path, and social mechanics. The system must be playable as a tabletop prototype. Submit a full mechanical specification, balance analysis with spreadsheet modeling, playtest results from at least 4 sessions, and a presentation to the class.
objectives: Synthesize all course concepts into a cohesive, original game system design
Achieve functional balance across asymmetric roles using quantitative modeling and iterative playtesting
Design economic, progression, and social systems that integrate without creating degenerate strategies
Present complex system designs clearly and defend balance decisions with data
deliverables: Complete mechanical specification document (10-15 pages) with formal notation for all systems, interactions, and balance parameters
Interaction matrix and dependency diagram showing all system connections
Balance analysis spreadsheet with cost curves, win rate simulations, and economy modeling
Playable tabletop prototype with all components
Playtest log from at least 4 sessions documenting balance observations, degenerate strategies discovered, and changes made
12-minute class presentation with slides, live demonstration, and Q&A

criteria: At least 3 asymmetric roles with distinct mechanics, goals, and play experiences
Economic system uses sources, sinks, and converters with sustainable flow rates
Progression system maintains engagement without creating power creep or mid-game valley
Social mechanics encourage meaningful interaction between roles
Balance analysis demonstrates roughly equal win rates across roles with supporting data
Cost curves are plotted and outliers addressed
No degenerate strategies remain unaddressed in the final version
Interaction matrix correctly identifies and manages all system connections
At least 4 playtest sessions with documented observations and data collection
Evidence of meaningful iteration between sessions driven by playtest findings
Stress testing attempted to find degenerate strategies
Final version demonstrates measurable improvement over initial version
Mechanical specification is precise enough for someone else to implement
Presentation is clear, well-structured, and within time limit
Design decisions are defended with data and reasoning, not just intuition
Honest post-mortem acknowledges remaining issues and lessons learned
Comprehensive, original system design synthesizing all course concepts
Quantitatively validated balance across asymmetric roles
Thorough playtesting with evidence-driven iteration
Professional presentation with data-supported design defense

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 5, 8, 11, 14) assessing theoretical knowledge of mechanics, feedback loops, balance, probability, economics, progression, emergence, competitive/cooperative design, asymmetry, social mechanics, player modeling, and metagame design

lab_assignments.description: Weekly design exercises, in-class mechanical analysis activities, system diagramming practice, and participation in collaborative playtesting sessions

major_assignments.description: Mechanical Systems Analysis and Redesign (150 pts), Emergent Mechanics Tabletop Game (150 pts), and Complete Mechanical System Design with Balance Analysis (200 pts)

midterm_examination.description: Take-home midterm covering weeks 1-8: mechanics taxonomy, feedback loops, balance theory, reward systems, probability, economics, progression, and emergence

final_project.description: Final mechanical system design with asymmetric roles, balance analysis, playtesting evidence, and in-class presentation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. In-class game analysis exercises, collaborative system modeling, and peer playtests cannot be replicated outside of class. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade by one half-letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments are penalized 10% per day late. The emergent mechanics game must be submitted on time as in-class presentation slots cannot be rescheduled. The final project presentation date is firm.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts and collaborative brainstorming are encouraged. Written analyses and the midterm must be individual work. The emergent tabletop game may be done in pairs if approved by the instructor. The final project is done in teams of 2-3.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All written work must be original. External sources, including published game analyses and balance data, must be cited. Fabricating playtest data or presenting another designer's mechanics as your own will result in a zero and academic referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students should have access to a spreadsheet application for quantitative modeling and a diagramming tool for system visualization. Basic prototyping supplies (cards, dice, tokens) are needed for tabletop projects. No programming is required.
